topic: Artistic Culture in the Hispanic World
subject: spanish

items.q: Which mood follows 'Espero que' (I hope that)?
items.why: Students use indicative for what feels certain. Expressions of hope, wish, doubt, emotion + QUE trigger SUBJUNCTIVE: Espero que..., Quiero que..., Dudo que..., Es importante que... Common in future plans/aspirations writing.

items.q: How do you say 'My sister is embarrassed' in Spanish?
items.correct: Mi hermana tiene vergüenza / está avergonzada.
items.why: Notorious mistake when discussing family/relationships. 'Embarazada' = PREGNANT, not embarrassed. To say embarrassed: tener vergüenza or estar avergonzado/a. Saying your sister is pregnant when you mean embarrassed causes laughter.

items.q: Which mood follows 'quiero que' (I want that)?
items.why: Students use indicative after que. Expressions of wish, emotion, doubt, or impersonal expressions trigger SUBJUNCTIVE: 'Quiero que VENGAS' (not 'que vienes'). Different subjects require que + subjunctive.

items.q: What is the voseo form of 'tú sabes'?
items.why: Correct answer: vos sabés. “vos sabes” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: Roughly how many million live in Mexico?
items.why: It's 130m. “80m” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: In what year did the Cuban Revolution triumph?
items.why: Exact answer: 1959. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
